english: Baked Eggplant with Mozzarella and Tomato

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Parmigiana di Melanzane as a composite baked dish. FOODON_03302114 (eggplant food product) is used as the closest parent representing the primary ingredient.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Parmigiana di Melanzane as a prepared composite dish. A00GJ (Aubergines/eggplants) is used as the closest parent based on the primary ingredient. The dish also contains mozzarella, tomato sauce, and Parmesan.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Parmigiana di Melanzane as a specific prepared food item.

preparation_method: fried (eggplant slices pre-fried or grilled before layering)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Parmigiana di Melanzane is approximately 300 g, comprising several layered slices of baked eggplant with mozzarella, tomato sauce, and Parmesan cheese.

nutrient_source.title: Melanzane alla parmigiana — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate; cross-referenced with Italian food composition data (CREA Banca Dati di Composizione degli Alimenti)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service; CREA (Council for Agricultural Research and Economics, Italy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Parmigiana di Melanzane as a complete dish. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy ingredient data (eggplant raw, mozzarella whole milk, tomato sauce, Parmesan) combined with standard Italian recipe proportions (~50% eggplant, ~20% mozzarella, ~20% tomato sauce, ~5% Parmesan, ~5% olive oil) and cross-referenced against CREA Italian food composition database entries for melanzane alla parmigiana. Energy and macros are consistent with published Italian dietary reference values for this dish (~100–130 kcal/100g range).

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Eggplant-based dishes and vegetable-dominant baked casseroles — analogous estimate based on eggplant (GI ~15) and tomato sauce composite; University of Sydney GI Database reference for non-starchy vegetable dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: Parmigiana di Melanzane does not appear as a named entry in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 35 based on: (1) eggplant has a very low GI (~15, University of Sydney GI Database for non-starchy vegetables); (2) tomato sauce has a low GI (~35); (3) mozzarella and Parmesan contribute negligible glycemic load; (4) the dish is predominantly non-starchy vegetables with moderate fat and protein that further blunt glycemic response. A composite GI of 35 is a conservative low estimate consistent with published values for similar baked vegetable and cheese dishes. GL per serving = (35 × 7.5 × 300) / (100 × 100) = 7.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg/100g); a 300g serving delivers approximately 960 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful of portion size, as a standard restaurant serving of Parmigiana di Melanzane can contribute approximately 960 mg sodium (~42% of the 2300 mg daily limit), particularly when prepared with salted cheese and seasoned tomato sauce; requesting reduced-salt preparation or limiting to a half-portion is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and moderate carbohydrate content (7.5 g/100g); dietary fibre (2.1 g/100g) and fat slow glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Parmigiana di Melanzane has a low estimated glycemic index (~35) and a moderate glycemic load (~7.9 per 300g serving), making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the high fibre from eggplant and the fat from mozzarella and olive oil further attenuate postprandial glucose response, though total carbohydrate intake across the meal should still be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from mozzarella and Parmesan (~3.1 g/100g); beneficial olive oil monounsaturated fats partially offset risk; sodium load is also relevant
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While Parmigiana di Melanzane contains beneficial olive oil and eggplant (a source of antioxidants including nasunin), the saturated fat from mozzarella and Parmesan (~3.1 g/100g, ~9.3 g per 300g serving) and the sodium content (~960 mg per serving) are relevant concerns for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing reduced-fat cheese variants and smaller portions can improve the cardiovascular profile of this dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Parmigiana di Melanzane (also known as Eggplant Parmigiana or Aubergine Parmigiana) is a classic Southern Italian baked dish originating from the Campania and Sicily regions of Italy. It is prepared by layering slices of eggplant (aubergine) — which are typically salted to draw out moisture, then fried or grilled — with tomato sauce (passata), fresh mozzarella cheese, grated Parmesan cheese, and fresh basil leaves, before baking the assembled dish in an oven until golden and bubbling. The result is a rich, savoury, and deeply flavourful casserole with a tender, silky texture from the eggplant, a tangy sweetness from the tomato sauce, and a creamy, slightly salty character from the melted cheeses. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~115 kcal/100g), meaningful protein from the dairy (~5.8 g/100g), and beneficial dietary fibre from the eggplant (~2.1 g/100g). The glycemic index is low (~35), making it suitable for blood glucose management. Sodium content is moderate to high (~320 mg/100g) due to the cheese and seasoned sauce, which is relevant for hypertension management. The dish is naturally gluten-free and vegetarian. It is commonly served as a main course (secondo piatto) or a substantial side dish (contorno) in Italian cuisine, and is widely available in Italian restaurants globally, including in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (USDA FoodData Central, CREA) provides a complete named entry for Parmigiana di Melanzane as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy ingredient data using standard Italian recipe proportions and cross-referenced with CREA Italian food composition data. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for eggplant and similar non-starchy vegetable dishes. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input 'Grains and staples' to 'Vegetables' as Parmigiana di Melanzane is a vegetable-dominant baked dish with eggplant as the primary ingredient. 2. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy ingredient composition data using standard recipe proportions (~50% eggplant, ~20% mozzarella, ~20% tomato sauce, ~5% Parmesan, ~5% olive oil by weight) and cross-referenced with CREA Italian food composition database. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported for this dish in the source databases consulted. 4. selenium_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source databases for this composite dish. 5. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Parmigiana di Melanzane was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. The dish is naturally gluten-free in its traditional form (no breadcrumbs); some restaurant variants may include breadcrumbs, which would introduce gluten — the is_gluten_free flag reflects the traditional preparation. 7. Halal status is assumed based on the absence of pork or alcohol in the traditional recipe; individual restaurant preparations may vary. 8. This dish is Italian cuisine, not Singaporean; therefore the multilingual naming rules for Singaporean dishes (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) do not apply. Local names are not required for Italian cuisine under Project Veritas v2 multilingual rules.
